summary: Joel Spolsky enthusiastically recommends Firefox 0.9 as a replacement for Internet Explorer. He argues that Firefox offers superior security through popup blocking, better usability through tabbed browsing, and represents a chance to break Microsoft's browser monopoly. He acknowledges Microsoft won the browser market fairly by making a better product, but criticizes them for stagnating IE development out of fear that web applications would undermine Windows.
key_points: Firefox offers practical security advantages over IE, including popup blocking and resistance to spyware installation prompts
Tabbed browsing represents a genuine usability improvement that keeps users after they initially switch for security
Microsoft deliberately stagnated IE development because web-based applications threatened the Windows platform's relevance
Competition in browsers could eventually make web-based applications competitive with native Windows applications
money_quotes: I switched for the popup blocking but I stayed for the tabbed browsing.

Microsoft took over the browser market fair and square by making a better product, but they were so afraid that Web-based applications would eliminate the need for Windows that they locked the IE team in a dark dungeon and they haven't allowed improvements to IE for several years now.
Now Firefox is the better product and there's a glimmer of hope that one day DHTML will actually improve to the point where web-based applications are just as good as Windows-based applications.
You'll get fewer viruses and you'll get no annoying popups asking you if you want to install lame spyware that will ruin your computer forcing a complete reinstall.
key_insight: Microsoft's fear of web applications replacing Windows led them to deliberately stagnate browser innovation, creating an opening for Firefox to become the better product.
